agent1_snippets.title: Sickle Cell Anemia -- Evaluation -- Acute Complications in Patients with SCA
agent1_snippets.content: Aplastic crises: It is usually precipitated by parvovirus B-19 and is defined as a rapid drop in Hb at least 3 to 6 gm/dL below the baseline. Patients present with severe fatigue, anemia, shortness of breath, and even syncope. Blood counts show severely low hemoglobin with near-absent reticulocytes. Bone marrow biopsy shows arrest in the pro-normoblast stage in patients with acute parvovirus infections. [9]

agent1_snippets.title: Sickle Cell Anemia -- Histopathology
agent1_snippets.content: In patients with SCA, peripheral blood smear shows elongated RBC with tapering ends that look like a sickle (also called drepanocytes). Additional findings are present in a few patients. Howell-Jolly bodies- Remnants of DNA are seen in the RBC and commonly seen in patients in whom the spleen has been removed. Therefore, patients with SCA have auto-splenectomy. Target cells (Leptocytes)- Most commonly seen in patients with Thalassemia. They are seen frequently in sickle-thalassemia syndromes and are sometimes noted in patients with SCA. Polychromatic cells - these are reticulocytes that signify marrow response to hemolysis. Nucleated red blood cells can sometimes be visible on the peripheral smear.

agent1_snippets.title: Sickle Cell Disease (Archived) -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: In the United States, most patients with sickle cell disease are diagnosed with newborn or prenatal screening. The diagnosis is based on hemoglobin electrophoresis that quantifies the types of hemoglobin and detects the various hemoglobinopathies. Among patients presenting with symptoms, the usual lab evaluation consists of complete blood count (CBC) with differential, reticulocyte count, complete metabolic panel, LDH level, bilirubin level and determination of blood type, and crossmatch for possible transfusion therapy. Appropriate cultures including that of blood should be obtained if the infectious process is suspected. [8] [9] [10] [11]

agent1_snippets.title: Histology, Red Blood Cell -- Microscopy, Light
agent1_snippets.content: A bite cell has a semi-circular indentation as if someone took a bite from it. Echinocytes are erythrocytes with uniform projections all equidistant apart. Schistocyte is a term that refers to an RBC fragment that has been sheared. A sickle cell is an RBC with a crescentic shape. Spherocytes are erythrocytes that have no central pallor and are uniformly red throughout. Stomatocytes have a linear central clearing rather than a circular one. Target cells have a red center with a central clearing. Teardrop cells taper on one end. One last group of erythrocyte descriptors evaluated with light microscopy is inclusion bodies. These are cytoplasmic or nuclear findings that give clinical clues. For example, Heinz bodies are small round masses seen with supravital or Heinz stains that indicate hemolysis. Howell-Jolly bodies are solid, large, round masses found in the hemoglobin of the cell, are visualized with Wright-stain, and have many clinical significances. Pappenheimer bodies are multiple, small blue inclusion bodies throughout the entire RBC, indicating iron overload, hyposplenism, or myelodysplasia. Finally, basophilic stippling is blue/purple granules seen in thalassemia, lead poisoning, and other pathologies. [5]

agent1_snippets.title: Poikilocytosis -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: Poikilocytocis due to inherited causes like thalassemia or sickle cell anemia may require treatment for a long duration, depending on their clinical variants (homozygous or heterozygous). Genetic diseases, generally, require lifelong monitoring with the treatment of periodic disease exacerbations. These cases may require blood transfusions, or in some cases of poikilocytosis, a bone marrow transplant may be necessary to treat their condition. People with other causes of poikilocytosis, like liver disease (with target cells or acanthocytes), should be treated accordingly. Some may require a liver transplant, while patients with sepsis or serious infections (may have schistocytes) may need antibiotics.

agent1_snippets.title: Pernicious Anemia -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: A peripheral blood smear may show macro-ovalocytes, hypersegmented neutrophils, and anisopoikilocytosis. Although hypersegmented neutrophils are often considered a hallmark of megaloblastic anemia, they are not specific, as they can be seen in other types of anemia, like iron deficiency anemia. Hypersegmented neutrophils typically precede macrocytosis and anemia; however, in advanced disease, they may be rare or absent.

agent1_snippets.content: Sickle cell disease : A hallmark finding in sickle cell disease is the presence of sickle-shaped red blood cells. These cells have a crescent or "sickle" shape due to the abnormal Hgb S in individuals with this genetic disorder. Another characteristic feature of sickle cell disease is Howell-Jolly bodies, which are small nuclear remnants within RBCs. Howell-Jolly bodies are typically seen in these individuals due to functional asplenia or hyposplenism.

agent1_snippets.title: Sickle Cell Hepatopathy -- Pathophysiology -- Viral hepatitis
agent1_snippets.content: Under chronic manifestations, patients with sickle cell disease who have multiple transfusions are at a higher risk for viral hepatitis B or C, depending on the transfusion protocols in their location and vaccination status. [6] Patients who received blood transfusions after 1992 are at a much lower risk of hepatitis. [4] These infections are primarily chronic and occur in the background of sickle cell hepatopathy, as evidenced by histology that reveals sinusoidal dilatation, hyperplastic Kupffer cells (conducted erythrophagocytosis), necrosis of the hepatic parenchyma, along with hepatitis infection, characterized by piecemeal and focal hepatocyte necrosis, portal tract inflammation, and lymphoid follicles. [13]

agent1_snippets.title: Pernicious Anemia -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: Follow-up serology will include anti-IF antibodies with or without antiparietal cell antibodies. Anti-IF antibodies are 40 to 60% sensitive in detecting pernicious anemia, with the rate of positivity rising with disease progression. The specificity of anti-IF antibody testing is almost 100%. Although antiparietal cell antibodies are present in 90% of patients with pernicious anemia, they are less specific than anti-IF antibodies. Notably combining the two has been shown to significantly increase their diagnostic performance for pernicious anemia (73% sensitivity and 100% specificity). [28] [33] A serum cobalamin of <200 ng/L plus the presence of anti-IF antibodies confirms a diagnosis of pernicious anemia.

agent1_snippets.title: Splenic Sequestration Crisis -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Splenic sequestration is a feared complication of sickle cell anemia that primarily affects young children. It is an acute drop in hemoglobin of 2 g/dL accompanied by splenomegaly. The spleen is at particular risk for complications from sickle cell anemia due to its role as a filter of the blood. The spleen is composed of three areas; white pulp, red pulp, and a transitional zone, and each plays a role in the immune system. A minority of blood flow entering the spleen (approximately 10%) is directed outside of the vessels into the parenchyma of the red pulp, where the red blood cells (RBCs) become exposed to reticuloendothelial cells such as macrophages which clear away abnormal cells or other pathogens. [1] To re-enter the vascular system, the RBCs must squeeze through narrow slits in the endothelium of the venous sinuses. The organization of the white pulp of the spleen is predominantly T-cell periarteriolar sheaths and follicles, consisting of B cells. These B cells are critical in the production of IgM antibodies capable of opsonizing encapsulated bacteria such as Streptococcus pneumoniae, Neisseria meningitidis, and Haemophilus influenza type B. Among children with sickle cell anemia, the spleen gradually loses function and size over the first five years of life due to repeated episodes of autoinfarction and scarring. [2] Children with less severe variants of sickle cell disease such as hemoglobin SC disease or sickle cell-beta thalassemia may have preserved some splenic function into adulthood, and studies have shown that treatment with hydroxyurea can preserve and even result in some recovery of splenic function. [2]

agent1_snippets.title: Sickle Cell Hepatopathy -- Differential Diagnosis
agent1_snippets.content: Under sickle cell hepatopathy, chronic viral hepatitis, transfusion-related iron overload, and sickle cell cholangiopathy should be considered in a more chronic hepatopathy that can present with persistently elevated transaminases. Moreover, hereditary hemochromatosis and other liver conditions, including autoimmune hepatitis, primary biliary cirrhosis, and primary sclerosing cholangitis, must be excluded. Other etiologies, including infections (eg, bacterial or fungal), should also be considered.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2276
agent2_snippets.content: The category of autoimmune disorders (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية) encompasses several distinct conditions, including التهاب المفاصل (arthritis), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد (cold‑agglutinin hemolytic anemia), and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation). Each of these diseases is characterized by an inappropriate immune response that targets the body’s own tissues, leading to joint inflammation, destruction of red blood cells, or inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing that these diverse clinical entities share a common autoimmune basis aids clinicians in diagnosing, monitoring, and applying immunomodulatory therapies across this spectrum of immune‑mediated illnesses.

This grouping reflects the shared underlying involvement of the immune system in the pathogenesis of these diverse diseases, whether through direct autoimmune attack on blood‑forming tissues, inflammatory processes affecting joints, or vascular inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing these entities as members of the immune/autoimmune disorder spectrum helps clinicians consider common immunologic mechanisms, guide diagnostic evaluation, and select therapeutic strategies that target immune dysregulation across these seemingly disparate clinical presentations.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1774
agent2_snippets.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, the Arabic term for sickle cell disease. This condition is clinically associated with قَرحَة (ulcer), reflecting the propensity for vaso‑occlusive events and tissue ischemia that can lead to ulcer formation. A key manifestation of داء الكريات المنجلية is نقص الأكسجين في الدم (blood oxygen deficiency or hypoxemia), which results from impaired red‑cell flexibility and consequent reduced oxygen delivery. Recognizing that sickle cell anemia (داء الكريات المنجلية) can present with ulcers and low blood‑oxygen levels is important for comprehensive patient assessment and targeted management.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2177
agent2_snippets.content: Aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل), arthritis (التهاب المفاصل), acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب), autoimmune‑mediated hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية), and coronary arteritis (التهاب الشريان التاجي) are all classified as members of the autoimmune disorder group (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية SMQ). These conditions share a common pathophysiology in which the immune system mistakenly attacks self‑tissues, leading to hematologic abnormalities in the various anemias and inflammatory damage in joints and coronary arteries. Recognizing this shared autoimmune basis guides clinicians toward similar diagnostic approaches—such as autoantibody testing—and therapeutic strategies, including immunosuppressive or immunomodulatory treatments, while emphasizing the need to monitor for systemic involvement across both blood‑forming and vascular systems.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 713
agent2_snippets.content: The category “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)” encompasses a range of hematologic and inflammatory conditions, including فقر الدم اللا تنسجي (aplastic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية (hemolytic anemia not caused by autoimmunity), التهاب المفاصل (arthritis), and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation). These entities are grouped together because they involve immune system dysregulation, whether through autoantibody‑mediated attacks, cell‑mediated immune injury, or broader immune‑related inflammatory processes. While most of the listed disorders are driven by autoimmune mechanisms, the non‑autoimmune hemolytic anemia is included in the classification for organizational purposes despite its different pathogenesis. Recognizing that these diverse diseases share an underlying immune component helps clinicians identify common diagnostic pathways and consider immunomodulatory therapies across different organ systems, thereby improving patient assessment and management.

Aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل) is considered an autoimmune disorder, as are autoimmune hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية) and cold‑type hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد). agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1768
agent2_snippets.content: Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is a specific form of فقر الدم (anemia) that arises rapidly after substantial blood loss, leading to a sudden decrease in circulating red blood cells and hemoglobin levels. As a subtype of anemia, it shares the fundamental characteristic of reduced oxygen‑carrying capacity of the blood, but its acute onset distinguishes it from chronic or nutritional anemias. Prompt recognition and treatment of acute posthemorrhagic anemia are essential in patient care, as rapid restoration of intravascular volume and red cell mass—through fluid resuscitation, blood transfusion, and control of the bleeding source—are required to prevent tissue hypoxia, organ dysfunction, and hemodynamic instability. Understanding that acute posthemorrhagic anemia falls under the broader category of فقر الدم helps clinicians classify the condition, anticipate its pathophysiology, and apply appropriate therapeutic strategies within the overall framework of anemia management.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1385
agent2_snippets.content: The SMQ category of immune disorders/autoimmune comprises a diverse group of hematologic and rheumatologic conditions. Within this category, aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل) is recognized as an autoimmune-mediated failure of bone‑marrow production. Non‑regenerative anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي) is also classified as an autoimmune disorder in which the marrow does not adequately replace red blood cells. Acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب) and its cold‑agglutinin subtype (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد) are both autoimmune hemolytic processes in which antibodies target red blood cells, leading to their premature destruction. In addition, inflammatory arthritis (التهاب المفاصل) is included as an autoimmune joint disease characterized by chronic inflammation and tissue damage. Together, these entities illustrate how autoimmune mechanisms can affect both blood formation and joint health, underscoring the importance of recognizing their shared immunologic basis for accurate diagnosis and coordinated patient management.

agent2_snippets.content: Anemia associated with chronic kidney disease is placed within the “Other deficiency anemias” group, which also comprises folate‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات), vitamin B12‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل), and iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات). Within this category are several sub‑entities: non‑megaloblastic (aplastic) anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي), acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب), autoimmune hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية), cold‑type hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد), and non‑immune hemolytic anemia (فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية). Although thalassemia (الثلاسيميا) is a hemoglobinopathy rather than a classic deficiency anemia, it is organized under the same “Other deficiency anemias” heading, and sickle‑cell retinopathy (اعتلال الشبكية بالخلايا المنجلية) has been linked to thalassemia, indicating a retinal vascular complication of this hemoglobin disorder.
